summary: DHH reflects on being forced to drink Pepsi Max after his company cut its soda service, and the irrational guilt he feels as a Coca-Cola brand loyalist. He recognizes that his discomfort comes entirely from corporate branding and marketing rather than any real preference. He acknowledges Coca-Cola's controversial corporate history and admits he should be smarter than to fall for brand loyalty.
key_points: Corporate brand loyalty creates irrational emotional responses that override rational thinking
Coca-Cola's marketing machine has successfully turned consumers into 'brand zealots' despite the company's questionable corporate behavior
Self-awareness about being manipulated by branding doesn't automatically free you from its effects
money_quotes: Drinking that stuff probably wouldn't be so bad if it wasn't for this strange force demanding I hate the brand, and making me feel dirty when I proced to consume it anyway.
The brain-washing machine they call Coca Cola have done an excellent job on yours truely.

All those attractive commercials of people experiencing adventure and slogans about "life tastes god" have converted me into a brand zealot for a company with alledged connections to the CIA, obstruction of justice and bribery.
I should be smarter than this.
key_insight: Brand loyalty is a form of corporate brainwashing that persists even when you're fully aware of both its irrationality and the company's ethical failures.
